subject: Science
question_text: Observe the pictures A and B given in Fig. 13.1 carefully. Which of the following statement is correct for the above given pictures?
options: In A, cars 1 and 2 will come closer and in B, cars 3 and 4 will come closer.
In A, cars 1 and 2 will move away from each other and in B, cars 3 and 4 will move away.
In A, cars 1 and 2 will move away and in B, 3 and 4 will come closer to each other.
In A, cars 1 and 2 will come closer to each other and in B, 3 and 4 will move away from each other.
answer_key: In A, cars 1 and 2 will come closer to each other and in B, 3 and 4 will move away from each other.
answer_explanation: Unlike poles (N and S) attract each other, causing the cars to come closer. Like poles (N and N, or S and S) repel each other, causing the cars to move away.

subject: Science
question_text: The arrangement to store two magnets is shown by figures (a), (b), (c) and (d) in Fig. 13.2. Which one of them is the correct arrangement?
answer_explanation: For storing a pair of bar magnets securely, they should be placed parallel with their opposite poles on the same side. A piece of wood separates them, and soft iron pieces (keepers) are placed across their ends.

subject: Science
question_text: Three magnets A, B and C were dipped one by one in a heap of iron filing. Fig. 13.3 shows the amount of the iron filing sticking to them. The strength of these magnets will be
options: A>B>C
A<B<C
A=B=C
A<B>C
answer_key: A>B>C
answer_explanation: The amount of iron filings attracted to a magnet indicates its magnetic strength. Since A attracts the maximum filings, its strength is greatest, followed by B, and C attracts the least.

subject: Science
question_text: North pole of a magnet can be identified by
options: Another magnet having its poles marked as North pole and South pole.
Another magnet no matter whether the poles are marked or not.
Using an iron bar.
Using iron filings.
answer_key: Another magnet having its poles marked as North pole and South pole.
answer_explanation: Using the property of repulsion, you can identify the north pole. The marked North pole of another magnet will repel the unknown North pole.
provenance_excerpt: North pole of a magnet can be identified by

subject: Science
question_text: A bar magnet is immersed in a heap of iron filings and pulled out. The amount of iron filling clinging to the
options: North pole is almost equal to the south pole.
North pole is much more than the south pole.
North pole is much less than the south pole.
Magnet will be same all along its length.
answer_key: North pole is almost equal to the south pole.
answer_explanation: The magnetic strength is maximum and practically equal at both poles of a bar magnet. Hence, the amount of iron filings attached will be almost equal at both ends.

subject: Science
question_text: Paheli and her friends were decorating the class bulletin board. She dropped the box of stainless steel pins by mistake. She tried to collect the pins using a magnet. She could not succeed. What could be the reason for this?
answer_key: Stainless steel is generally non-magnetic (especially austenitic stainless steel often used for pins). Hence, it does not get attracted by a magnet.
provenance_excerpt: Paheli and her friends were decorating the class bulletin board. She dropped the box of stainless steel pins by mistake.

subject: Science
question_text: How will you test that ‘tea dust’ is not adulterated with iron powder?
answer_key: Move a magnet through or over the tea dust. If the tea dust is adulterated with iron powder, the magnetic iron particles will get attracted and stick to the magnet, leaving the pure non-magnetic tea dust behind.
provenance_excerpt: How will you test that ‘tea dust’ is not adulterated with iron powder?

subject: Science
question_text:
Boojho dipped a bar magnet in a heap of iron filings and pulled it out. He found that iron filings got stuck to the magnet as shown in Fig. 13.4.
(i) Which regions of the magnet have more iron filings sticking to it?
(ii) What are these regions called?
answer_key:
(i) The two ends of the bar magnet have the maximum iron filings sticking to them.
(ii) These regions are called the magnetic poles (North and South poles).
provenance_excerpt: Boojho dipped a bar magnet in a heap of iron filings and pulled it out. He found that iron filings got stuck to the magn

subject: Science
question_text:
Four identical iron bars were dipped in a heap of iron filings one by one. Fig. 13.5 shows the amount of iron filings sticking to each of them.
(a) Which of the iron bar is likely to be the strongest magnet?
(b) Which of the iron bars is not a magnet? Justify your answer.
answer_key:
(a) The iron bar that has the maximum clustering of iron filings strictly at its ends is the strongest magnet (e.g., bar 'a').
(b) The iron bar that does not attract any iron filings at all (e.g., bar 'b') is certainly not a magnet. By definition, a magnet attracts magnetic materials.
provenance_excerpt: Four identical iron bars were dipped in a heap of iron filings one by one. Fig. 13.5 shows the amount of iron filings st

subject: Science
question_text: A toy car has a bar magnet laid hidden inside its body along its length. Using another magnet how will you find out which pole of the magnet is facing the front of the car?
answer_key: Bring the marked North pole of a known magnet near the front end of the toy car. If the car is repelled and moves away, the pole facing the front is the North pole (like poles repel). If the car is attracted, the front faces the South pole.

subject: Science
question_text: You are provided with two identical metal bars. One out of the two is a magnet. Suggest two ways to identify the magnet.
answer_key:
1. Repulsion test: Bring a known magnet's pole near the ends of both bars one by one. The bar that gets repelled at one of its ends is the magnet. The plain iron bar will only show attraction at both ends.
2. Suspension test: Suspend both bars freely using a thread. The bar that aligns itself steadily in the North-South direction is the magnet.
answer_explanation: Methods to distinguish magnets from magnetic materials.
provenance_excerpt: You are provided with two identical metal bars. One out of the two is a magnet. Suggest two ways to identify the magnet.

subject: Science
question_text: Three identical iron bars are kept on a table. Two out of three bars are magnets. In one of the magnet the North-South poles are marked. How will you find out which of the other two bars is a magnet? Identify the poles of this magnet.
answer_key:
Take the marked magnet and bring its North pole close to each end of the other two bars.
1. The bar which shows repulsion at one of its ends is the second magnet. The end that is repelled by the North pole is its North pole, and the other end is its South pole.
2. The bar that is only attracted at both ends (showing no repulsion) is the ordinary iron bar.
answer_explanation: Advanced identification of magnets and poles.
provenance_excerpt: Three identical iron bars are kept on a table. Two out of three bars are magnets. In one of the magnet the North-South p

subject: Science
question_text: Describe the steps involved in magnetising an iron strip with the help of a magnet.
answer_key:
1. Place the iron strip flat on a table.
2. Take a bar magnet and place one of its poles (e.g., North) near one edge of the strip.
3. Without lifting the magnet, rub it along the length of the strip toward the other edge.
4. Lift the magnet, bring the same pole back to the starting point, and stroke it again in the same direction.
5. Repeat this stroking process 30-40 times to magnetise the iron strip.
provenance_excerpt: Describe the steps involved in magnetising an iron strip with the help of a magnet.

subject: Science
question_text: Fig. 13.6 shows a magnetic compass. What will happen to the position of its needle if you bring a bar magnet near it? Draw a diagram to show the effect on the needle on bringing the bar magnet near it. Also draw the diagram to show the effect when the other end of the bar magnet is brought near it.
answer_key: The position of the compass needle will deflect from its usual North-South alignment. This happens because the magnetic field of the bar magnet exerts a force on the magnetic needle. When the North pole of the magnet is brought near, the South pole of the needle points towards it. When the other end (South pole) of the magnet is brought near, the North pole of the needle will be attracted and point towards it.
answer_explanation: Effect of magnetic fields on a compass.
provenance_excerpt: Fig. 13.6 shows a magnetic compass. What will happen to the position of its needle if you bring a bar magnet near it? Dr

subject: Science
question_text: Suggest an activity to prepare a magnetic compass by using an iron needle and a bar magnet.
answer_key:
1. Magnetize an ordinary iron needle by repeatedly stroking it with one pole of a bar magnet in a single direction.
2. Fasten the magnetized needle onto or push it through a small piece of light material like a cork or foam.
3. Float the cork in a bowl or cup of water freely. As it comes to rest, the needle will orient itself in the geographic North-South direction, thereby acting as a simple compass.
provenance_excerpt: Suggest an activity to prepare a magnetic compass by using an iron needle and a bar magnet.

subject: Science
question_text: Boojho kept a magnet close to an ordinary iron bar. He observed that the iron bar attracts a pin as shown in Fig. 13.7. What inference could he draw from this observation? Explain.
answer_key: He can infer the phenomenon of magnetic induction. When an ordinary iron bar is kept very close to or touching a strong magnet, it temporarily acquires magnetic properties and behaves like a magnet itself. This induced magnetism allows the iron bar to attract the pin. Once the magnet is removed, the iron bar will lose its magnetism.
provenance_excerpt: Boojho kept a magnet close to an ordinary iron bar. He observed that the iron bar attracts a pin as shown in Fig. 13.7.

subject: Science
question_text: A bar magnet is cut into two pieces A and B, from the middle, as shown in Fig. 13.8. Will the two pieces act as individual magnets? Mark the poles of these two pieces. Suggest an activity to verify your answer.
answer_key: Yes, the two pieces A and B will act as individual, complete magnets. If the original magnet left end was N and right end S, then the cut right end of piece A becomes S, and the cut left end of piece B becomes N. Activity: Bring a known magnet to the cut ends; they will show distinct attraction and repulsion. Alternatively, dip the cut pieces into iron filings; the filings will cluster at the newly formed poles at the cut ends.

subject: Science
question_text: Suggest an arrangement to store a U shaped magnet. How is this different from storing a pair of bar magnets?
answer_key: To store a U-shaped magnet, you only need to place a single piece of soft iron (called a keeper) across its two nearby poles. In contrast, storing a pair of bar magnets requires placing them side by side with opposite poles on the same end, separating them with a wooden block, and placing two soft iron keepers across both pairs of ends.
provenance_excerpt: Suggest an arrangement to store a U shaped magnet. How is this different from storing a pair of bar magnets?
